speaker: interviewer
text: We were just talking about the safe place that you were when you went to the zoo one time. Did you go there with friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I guess I went there with friends, and possibly also with my sister. This is rather vague in my memory of it. I just remember that my father started an apartment, and possibly his practice as a dentist, in the-- well, in the suburb of Berlin, which was a very nice place called Buckow Markische Schweiz. This was a destination to come over the weekend to relax, to swim, to bathe. And I guess my father saw an opportunity in Buckow to become the local dentist. There was no dentist there. He started this in 1925. He married in 1927. I came along in 1928. And my father then also had a practice as a dentist in Berlin. The summers in Berlin where quite uncomfortable-- hot, muggy, humid. And so, because my father had this apartment in Buckow, we spent the summer months in Buckow, and I also went to school over there. My father stayed in Berlin because that was his main practice-- practiced dentistry during the week. Friday afternoon he took his one-cylinder car, the long trip to Buckow, which was cobble-- cobblestone highways-- streets, in that time. When he came to Buckow, about 6 o'clock in the afternoon on Friday, he came through-- we're talking a very small place. Right now Buckow has 1,400 inhabitants.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now?

speaker: interviewee
text: We visited just two years ago. Now. It might have been a little bit bigger, or it might have been a little bit smaller at that time. Anyway, when he came through main street, which was Berliner Strasse, he honked his horn, and everybody knew the dentist is, is in town. And they came, and waited in line to get the service.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we stayed. We stayed in Buckow over the summer months. So Friday night, all day Saturday, and I guess, maybe, Sunday morning, he practiced dentistry in Buckow. Sunday afternoon, or Sunday night, he jumped back in the car, left us in Buckow, and went back to Germany-- Germany-- back to Berlin, and stayed there, by the way, by himself. In Buckow was my mother, my father-- no, my mother, myself, my sister, sometimes my grandmother--

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose mother was this, your grandmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother was my mother's mother. And I think our maid was in both places, I don't remember. I found out in 1993, when I went back to Berlin and to Buckow, that we found a man, who is in his 90s, that remembered my father. And he said at the time, yes, I remember your father. My-- your father was the dentist. We all went to him. And when the Nazis took away his practice in 1935 or thereabouts, the practice was given to a woman dentist for her to practice there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of this man? Did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't. I don't. I don't. I don't know if it was given to me or not. I talked to him only, oh, maybe 15 minutes. This was very exciting to me. Going back to Buckow was very exciting because we spent a lot of time in Buckow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it look the same?

speaker: interviewee
text: It looked the same. Unfortunately the house that we lived in was gone-- practically the only two houses that were gone. I don't know why. I don't think the fighting went to Buckow. But I understand there are plans now to take this particular piece of ground and the next house over to build a hotel there, because it's becoming, again, a kind of place to go over the weekend. I do remember that right across the street from where we lived was a hotel, a small village hotel-- what are we talking-- about 10 rooms, a restaurant, a meeting hall in the back that had a movie theater in it, and the owner's son was my friend. And of course I went to all the movies.

speaker: interviewer
text: When we went back in 1993, I saw the place. I found the place. I went in, and I asked the owner-- was not the owner, it was just the-- one of the servers over there-- is there a movie theater, a movie screen in the back? No, I don't think so. There's a stage there. Can I look? Oh, of course. I went back there, I went on stage, and I still saw the hooks where the screen was attached to, so I knew I was in the right place. I also asked-- she immediately went to somebody, and he was the nephew of the former owner. And I asked him, tell me, was there ever a water pump in front of your hotel? He says, how did you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, 50 years ago I lived across the street, and that's where we got our water. We did not have any running water. We also didn't have any toilet. We had to go outside in the yard. So I knew I was in the right place at that time. And we had a very nice dinner there afterwards. And I talked to the-- talked to the-- to the guy that was the owner now, and I mentioned that my friend at that time-- and he says, that's my nephew, and he is still alive. He wasn't there at the time, so I couldn't meet him. We did lose-- well, the practice was closed in 1935, and I do remember one very awful incident that happened in Buckow, where they had a march of the Nazi troops going through one night with lit, burning candles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Torches, torches. And they were chanting, Juden raus, Juden raus.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which means?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which means, not kill the Jews, but evict the Jews. It could possibly have been that this was one of their favorite places, where Jews went over the weekend, too. But this, this is burned in my memory at one time that that happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's see. I was about six, six years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have Jewish as well as non-Jewish friends in Buckow?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I don't remember. I did go to the school over there, and I remember that the school was a lot more primitive than the school in Berlin. I remember that we wrote on slates-- not paper, but slates. And I also remember, vaguely, that of all places, Hindenburg, the Chancellor of Germany, visited that school. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know why.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you saw him? And I saw him, yeah. And I saw him. So did he speak to any--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: --any of the kids or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know why he was in the school. I guess maybe he gave a speech, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when your father's practice was taken away from him, did he continued to practice at all with his--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --former clients, patients?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened was, after they closed the practice, my father started working illegally, mostly for Jewish patients. And what he did was very dangerous. At that time the exodus started getting very serious. People started leaving Germany any which way they could. And of course, there was quite a number of them who got permission to go to England, got permission to go to Australia, got permission to go to the United States. But then of course what happened was, ah-ha, now that you're leaving, you have to leave your wealth, or most of your wealth, behind. Matter of fact, you had to turn in your gold, your silver, your diamonds.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you consider your family a well-off family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, my father, I think, was struggling in those years, from 1925 till his business was closed. He-- well, he wasn't a millionaire, but he wasn't poor, either. I would say just upper middle class. I would say upper middle class. Then, after he closed-- after the business was closed, ironically speaking, he was making more money than he ever did before because now he was working illegally. And what he mostly did was, he took and made dentures out of gold, pure gold, especially lower dentures, because the Jews could not take their jewelry out. So they melted the gold, and they made the dentures, especially the lower dentures, out of gold. Now, that's very obvious, right, but in those days dentures were made, not of-- not out of plastic, but they were made out of vulcanite, rubber. So he made the denture out of gold and covered it. The teeth were on top, and covered it, all around with vulcanite, and also the bottom, so the gold was hidden inside the denture. And I'm saying this, especially with lower dentures, because you can make them quite big. The upper ones would fall down, but the lower ones would stay in place. This was not something that you would eat with, this was just something you stuck in your mouth when you get across the border. Well, there was always quite a bit of waste gold, and my father, first of all, charged, of course, for that, and there was always something left over for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: People still had money to spend then, even though their jobs were take away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. Oh, yes. Yes. I do remember that particular thing. I do remember one incident then, that all this waste gold that my father then accumulated, he, of course, also tried to take out of Germany. He hid that gold on the inside of an electric iron. He took the insides out, and filled it full of gold. Now an iron is pretty heavy to start with, so it didn't make much difference how much gold-- there might have been some other places. OK, to jump ahead a little bit, when we got to Shanghai, our luggage, our suitcases-- these are heavy, big suitcases-- one of them was broken into, had a big hole in it. My father says, oh, my god, that is the piece of luggage where I hid the iron.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the iron missing?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was still in there. So-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So these changes happened rapidly here. What other changes happened to you? Do you-- do you remember things that specifically happened to you as a kid? Different ways people treated you or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. I spent a lot of time with my uncle. And then, of course, I went to the Kaliski School, where we were every day. And I was very much on the safe side at that particular time. This is why we're going to talk about that-- shall we talk about the Kaliski School--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: --why it became famous? The Kaliski School, like I said before, was owned by an American, and therefore was untouchable. And we stayed there and learned there. And it became afterwards known as the island [GERMAN], the island of refuge. And the Berlin government saw fit to kind of honor that school after the war. And in 1993 we were invited, by the Berlin government, to participate in the opening of a museum, a part of a museum exhibit of part of the relics, pictures, of that school. And there were roughly about 125 of us who came to that opening. And we were treated royally. We went back to the school. The school is still there. It is still a school. Matter of fact it was enlarged. We had a swimming pool. The swimming pool was covered up, and there is now another building there, which is also a school. And we were supposed to talk about our experiences in that school. I could not remember any of the students. I could not remember any of the teachers. I just-- you know, it just-- just drew a blank. The 125 pupils at that time, that came together again in Berlin, and were treated at that particular time at Hotel Kempinski, one of the hotels at Unter den Linden, for a get together which started at 4 o'clock in the afternoon. And we had coffee and wonderful cake and tortes. It lasted until about 10 o'clock at night. We got up and talked about it-- we were videoed at that time, too-- about our experience. And it turned out that most of the people that talked about the school, talked about the jokes that they played on their teachers. And when I got up, I said, you know, I thought I was the only bad boy in school. I thought I was the only one who played jokes on the teachers. And please, could anybody tell me whatever happened to the joke that I played on the teachers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What joke was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which was, yeah [LAUGHS] I got a hold of a baby pacifier, took the rubber tip off, stuck it on top of the cold water faucet, and turned the water on. And the balloon-- it became a balloon-- started to expand. And of course, I ran away. Well, at some time or another that balloon must have burst. What happened? Did anybody remember? No, nobody remembered.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, anyway, we-- we had a very good time at that meeting, and everybody talked about the jokes that they played. One of them was, one of the teachers apparently was very short. And the kids had a basket-- well, he had a basket where he put the chalks to write on the-- [LAUGHS] on the blackboard. So they took the basket and, instead of putting it lower down, they raised it up higher than he could reach. He left it there.

speaker: interviewee
text: One day they put a dead mouse in that basket, instead of the chalk. Well, he reached up there, of course, and got a hold of the-- of the mouse. He took the mouse, left it in the basket, took the basket and said, it seems like we have a dead mouse here. We will all go outside and bury the mouse. And they did. I understand they dug up the mouse and put it back the next day. These are just some of the jokes, you know. I mean, we were kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is when you lived in--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was when we lived in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Berlin, at the Kaliski School.

speaker: interviewer
text: Funny.

speaker: interviewee
text: I must have-- I must have learned something [LAUGHS] because, obviously, I'm not an idiot. But, like I said before, schooling was-- was-- was not one of my bright sides.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any religious schooling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, I did become Bar Mitzvahed in Shanghai. And, yes, we had religious classes in-- oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you consider your family, if you were to classify yourselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were reformed. We were reformed. And, as an aside, we did not go to services every Friday night. We did not keep kosher, neither did my grandmother. To be very blunt about it, we were Germans of Jewish persuasion, and we really didn't practice it. We-- yes, we did go to the High Holidays. And I must tell you, at that time, we were absolutely warned-- this during the Hitler period-- not to wear our best clothes when we went to the High Holiday services.

speaker: interviewer
text: Warned by who?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the Jewish rabbis.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because?

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't make a scene. Don't congregate in front of the synagogue in a great big group. Be inconspicuous. Don't flaunt it. You know, don't go in groups, kind of file in, and go out, and go home, and don't talk in front of the synagogue. Interestingly enough the synagogues, most of the synagogues in Germany where not facing the street. You usually had to go through an apartment house in the front, through a gate, and then the second [NON-ENGLISH], the second section of the house, that's where the synagogue was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of yours?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the big one, Oranienburger Strasse. This is the one that had the gold dome. This is the one that, of course, was burned, and this is the one also that got bombed during the bombings of Berlin. And this is the one that is now, I understand, completely restored by the Berlin government.

speaker: interviewer
text: So do you remember the events of November 9 and 10, in 1938?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kristallnacht? Yes, I do remember that. The reason I remember it is, we lived at that time at Bruckenstrasse facing the front, and right across the street from us where two businesses, one of them was a bakery.

speaker: interviewer
text: The bakery, [GERMAN]-- and I have-- could I say and aside right here? I remember because it was a bakery, and we got our hard rolls there every morning. We also, when we baked cakes, we mixed the cakes at home, then took the cake and went over there and had it baked in the oven. That was the way the fashion was.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I went back in 1993, I went back to Bruckenstrasse. Across the street was still the bakery. I went in there. It had a different name-- Schmidt, or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who owned the bakery when you were young?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the name of the bakery was, I found out later, [GERMAN]. I went into this bakery, and I said to the lady behind the counter, was this the bakery [GERMAN]? And right away she kind of became very standoffish and said, why do you want to know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Bruckenstrasse was in the Russian zone, and after the Russian zone became now just Berlin again, there is lots of people that have owned houses before the reunification, and there's a lot of trouble going on. So she like, who are you? Why do you want to know? And I said to her, the last time I came to your bakery was 50 years ago. I lived across the street. And I emigrated to China, and then to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I see. And then she became friendly and she said, no, no, this was not the bakery [GERMAN]. It was the bakery [GERMAN]. And she says, it's about time that you come back and buy some cake, which I did. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, right next to the bakery [GERMAN] was a hardware store. And the hardware store was owned by a [? Mr. Fuchs ?], F-U-X, or maybe C-H. He was Jewish. Kristallnacht, by 11 o'clock at night, 12 o'clock at night-- I don't remember what time it actually was-- the Nazis came, the Hitler Youth came-- the SS actually is what it was-- and smashed in the store. And, of course, looted it, by the way, too. Now could you see this from your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we saw it. We saw it right from-- from our window.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what you remember seeing.

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as emotion is concerned, I don't think I grasped the significance on it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because how old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, 1938, I was eight-- I was 10. '39-- 10, 11 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your parents or any--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were scared. We were scared, yes. And matter of fact, yes, especially my parents were scared because what could happen? My father was probably saying, hey, are we next? Are they going to come into our apartments? Are they going to beat us up? Are they going to take us away? They did, you know? They did broke into private homes. I think the reason they didn't come to us is because my father was not allowed to practice dentistry anymore, therefore the sign that he had in front of the house was gone. And apparently they didn't have a list with my father's name on it at that time, so nothing happened to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anything happen to any of your relatives, or any friends that you knew? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as I know, not.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yeah, yeah. The synagogue was, was, was burned at that time, and that was that. At that time going to services wasn't really on our mind. At that time what was on our mind is try to get everything together to try to get out of Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's talk about that. Your father had been trying to get your whole family out of Germany for a long time. When did you finally obtain a visa to leave--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and exit visa, and how? Do you want to tell me about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember my father talking about the idea of going to China, and as I said before, my mother being very opposed to the idea of going to Shanghai, China. Well, finally we got a very urgent letter from our relatives on my father's side, the Dessens, D-E-S-S-E-N, the Dessens, who lived in Shanghai already. I guess they went to Shanghai in about 1937. And it says, Dad-- or rather-- Dr. Chaim, you know, Hans, Heinrich, come, come, there is no time to lose. We have gotten for you an affidavit, for you and your family to come to Shanghai. You can come. You can finally come. Come, come, come. And at that time, as all other possibilities were exhausted, we started to go-- prepare ourselves to go to Shanghai.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was involved of going to Shanghai? You had to have exit visas, you had to have entrance visa-- well the entrance visa was taken care of. Now you have to get an exit visa to get out of Germany. Why don't we stop the tape here so we can continue on on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
